comments: Tommy lists the Juicy Lucy track as one of his all time favourite records.

sessions.details: Debut TX: details unknown. No known commercial release.
recorded 1963-07-02 for Pop Go The Beatles, first TX 1963-07-16. Available on Beatles Live At The BBC (Apple).
recorded 1969-08-04 for Symonds On Saturday, first TX 1969-08-10. Available on Something's Coming: The BBC Recordings 1969-1970 (Lilith).
recorded 1970-01-19 for Dave Lee Travis show, first TX 1970-01-25. Available on Something's Coming: The BBC Recordings 1969-1970 (Lilith).

track_listing.track: 'Whole Lotta Rosie (LP-If You Want Blood You've Got It)' (Atlantic)

track_listing.details: LP-On Through The Night; Mercury / Polygram
Lay Down Your Arms; Pop Go The Beatles Session

track_listing.track: (Tommy trails the following day's Rock On. It rebroadcast the final interview with John Lennon, who had been murdered four days previously)
'I Don't Wanna Go Home (LP-The Turn Of A Friendly Card)' (Arista)

'Kashmir (2x LP-Physical Graffiti)' (Swan Song) The previous week, TV had asked listeners to nominate their second favourite Zeppelin track (discounting the obvious Stairway To Heaven). The final five were:

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
This is TV on the Radio here. Thomas the Vance. And welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, our salute to John Lennon. Two tracks he recorded with the Beatles in 1963.
We have some tracks by Yes from 69 and 1970. And from 1980, a band called Chevy.
We've got some classic cuts by Queen, Juicy Lucy, Blue Oyster Cult, Motörhead and The Doors.
Down the Doors, some new stuff by Molly Hatchett and Alan Parsons,
your second most favorite Led Zeppelin track,
and of course our Friday Night Connection, the answer to last week's and a new one.
But this evening, we start with this.
Do you remember when that came out as a single?
[Music]
Absolutely mind-boggling.
The live version of Bohemian Rhapsody.
Especially for Patrick and Faith
who live at [redacted address]
in Tapton, Chesterfield
who now have a daughter by the name of Marie
Congratulations
Also, that track was for Tommy Hawks
who lives somewhere in the UK
And before that, of course, a real classic by ACDC
from their live album
The track was Whole Lotta Rosie
And that was for Dorothy
with Best Wishes and Lots of Love from Joe Hyde
who lives with his wife, who's Dorothy, in South Shield
Also for loads of other people
by the name of Rob and Rollo and Mike and Steg and Paul,
who live in and around Buckley in Llywyd in North Wales.
And for a gentleman by the name of Philip,
lots of love from Lisa, his sister, who lives in Aberdare.
And the ACDC track was also for the wife of a man by the name of Nick.
That's Nick Fairley, who lives in Airdrie in Bonny, Scotland.
Margaret, hi there.
Also Jason, Marina, Big Tony and George.
ACDC, still the best.
[Music]
10.14 is the first of four by Chevy.
That was Def Leppard from their album On Through the Night
[Music]
[Music]
and the track called Wasted.
For all the Edbangers at Bryanston School,
including Paul and Warren and Matt and Bob and Ben Cook
and also Tab and Gus and Ben Shepard and Richard and Jake,
little message here for somebody by the name of Dave,
who maybe has been banging away quietly in the corner up until now, it says here.
Hey, Dave.
Austin is an ex-inmate. He sends his best wishes.
There was also a Jimmy Norcott and his band called Mens Ria.
Bill Arie.
And for Neil and Stuart, who've recently moved from Fowlbridge, it says here.
Also Howard and Bumbo and Orgasm, Azzy and Garbridge.
And the classes of Fowlbridge and finally the Gig School Rockers.
The Gig School Rockers, of course, being those who attend Giggleswick School in Settle, North Yorkshire.
And before that, you heard the first of four by Chevy.
Track was Skybird.
And a happy birthday I have to wish to a gentleman by the name of Brian.
It was his 21st birthday on the 9th of December.
I have to play it from...
is it, I can't really, I think it's Helen and Stowe
and Sarah and Marion and Phil in Reading
and also, of course, Mr. Alan Parkin,
who all congregate in and around Bolton in Lancashire.
It's 24 minutes after 10 on the Friday Rock Show.
Here's something which is in mono, Stereo Freaks.
This is in mono because it really is rare.
I cannot find out when it was last broadcast.
It's a couple of tracks recorded by Yes in August 1969.
Have a listen.
They really were such an amazing band, weren't they?
That was recorded in August 1969.
It was by Yes, hasn't been broadcast for a heck of a long time.
Couple of cuts there, Every Day and Lookin' Around.
And I still think Yes are a great band,
even though they're now, if you like, bugled joined.
I was listening the other night to Drama at Home on my stereo headphones,
and I do think it's a good album,
as indeed do I think Mr. John Anderson is making his mark as a solo artist.
Right, here's something by Molly Hatchet.
Down in history as one of my all-time favorite tracks,
[Music]
the track was few and far between.
Few and far between come 19th birthdays.
It's the 19th birthday today of Mark Smith,
who lives at [redacted address], Bradford in West Yorkshire.
Happy birthday from Mark and Alison.
P.S. you're a great guy, it says here.
And P.P.S. hope your new car survives your driving.
driving and i hope you survive your 19th birthday it's 20 minutes now before 11 on the friday rock
show i'd like to read you a piece here from the beatles appreciation society magazine book march
1980. it's about a broadcast that was called pop go the beatles it was transmitted on the 16th of
july 1963 between 5 o'clock and 5 29 p.m on the light program as it was in those days without a
doubt it says here this is the most fascinating beatles radio appearance ever of the nine songs
recorded that day, of which only six were broadcast, seven of them were never to appear
on a Beatles disc. The range of these songs is so wide and varied that it illustrates perfectly
the Beatles' own influences, as well as their tremendous versatility. Carol was a Chuck Berry
rocker from August 1958, and Soldier of Love, Lay Down Your Arms in parenthesis, was a song first
performed by Arthur Alexander, his version being released in May 1962. It's anti-war lyrics,
no doubt striking an affinity with john lennon john lennon is the lead vocalist on both of those
tracks george is just coming up here with a letter in his hand you're looking very serious about it
[Music]
what is it george it's a letter from kathy of newport isle of wight who says paul's been to
the isle of wight and why haven't i well i haven't got a passport huh and and she says would you
Please sing a song for the 5th of St. Teresa convent ride, Isle of Wight.
So we'd like to feature John shouting Soldiers of Love.
All right.
Soldiers of Love was that track, and before that you heard Carol.
The Beatles was recorded in July 1963.
John Lennon was the lead vocalist.
Don't miss Rock on Saturday tomorrow around about 4.30,
or rather just after the 4.30 news
because we're going to be playing part of the very last radio interview
that Mr. Lennon gave, and he gave it to Radio 1.
It actually will be a rebroadcast of a program
that went out between 11.30 and 12.30 on Tuesday last.
Don't miss it, because it's a fitting tribute to a great guy.
The Alan Parsons Project from their latest album,
or if you like his latest album, The Turn of a Friendly Card
is the album title.
That track was called I Don't Wanna Go Home.
Before that, from Chevy, we had Rock On.
It is 4 minutes to 11 on the Friday Rock Show. It's time for the Friday Night Connection.
Right, the tracks we played you last work, week, work, last working week were
Turn to Stone from the Joe Warshall LP called So Far So Good.
Then we had Remember Tomorrow by Iron Maiden from the LP Iron Maiden.
And then it was Angels at My Gate by Manfred Mann's Earth Band.
The LP was called Angel Station and that is on the bronze label.
So the answer, Stone, Tone 2, Iron Maiden, and Manfred Mayer, who records for the Bronze label.
So if you like, Stone Age, Iron Age, Bronze Age.
Not a lot of correct answers at all this week, but a few.
And the one that we've chosen is from Martin Dykes, who lives at [redacted address].
I can hear the cheers from here.
Kewstoke Road, Wall, Western Supermare in Avon.
Martin, you get yourself a £10 record token. Congratulations.
now as everybody knows part of the deal is to put together your own friday night connection
three bits of music with a connection and or theme running through them i think this one is
particularly good dedicate one to the lady summertime sip babe need something to keep you
on now summertime sip babe need something to keep you cool better look out now though
Dave's got something for you, I'll tell you what it is
I'm your ice cream man, stop me when I'm passing by
On my mind, I'm your ice cream man, stop me when I'm passing by
See now all my flavors are guaranteed to satisfy
Hold on a second there
I gotta put my banana, see cups
All flavors and push-ups too
I'm your ice cream man, baby, stop me when I'm passing by
See now all my flavors are guaranteed to satisfy
Hold on one more
Well I'm usually passing by just about 11 o'clock
I never stop, I'm usually passing by just around 11 o'clock
And if you let me cool you one time, you'll be my regular stop
Alright boys
I put my vanilla flavors in for you to have your ice cream made
Stop you in a bad seat
Now all my flavors get each satisfied.
In Dykes of Western Supermare, you've got taste.
That's our Friday Night Connection for tonight.
Three bits of music excellently chosen by Mr. Dykes of Western Supermare and Avon.
Do you think you know what the connection is?
If you think that you do, then it's in there somewhere.
In actual fact, it's not difficult.
I just thought the choice of music was excellent.
Jot it down on a postcard.
Any form of communication you care to choose and or use.
And send it to the Friday Night Connection.
The Friday Night Connection, the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London W1A 4WW.
Put together your own Friday Night Connection as well.
If you're the winner, you get a £10 record token.
Many thanks to everybody who wrote in last week when I asked you to send in your, if you like, second favourite Led Zeppelin track.
Because everybody knows that the most favourite Led Zeppelin track of all time is Stay Away To Heaven.
Thanks to everybody that wrote in, we appreciate it.
And I'm not being patronising when I say that.
At number five, we had No Quarter.
At number four, Dazed and Confused.
At number three, Whole Lotta Love.
At number two, Since I've Been Loving You.
And at number one...
Just listening to that track again,
sort of really does sort of hammer home the fact
that Bonham could not have been replaced in many, many ways.
He was such a distinctive drummer.
Led Zeppelin and your second most favourite Led Zeppelin track
from Physical Graffiti.
The track, of course, Kashmir.
And that's the one you voted your second favourite.
Just a few mentions for all people who did write in.
Mike Newbold, thank you, of Wotton Bassett in Wiltshire.
And also a lady by the name of Susie, who's at Vincent's Club in Oxford.
Another lady, Karen Page, who's at Kashmir.
That's the name of her house.
Adelston, which is in Surrey, I think.
Lesley-Anne of Aberdeen, up there at [redacted address].
Another lady by the name of Page.
Carol Page, she says, no relation to Jimmy.
Carol lives in Windermere.
And also Steve Denham in Manchester.
Dino and all his mates in Wyverton in Boston
and a gentleman by the name of Phil who wrote this card.
He says, see you at Walsall Town Hall on Saturday night
along with Diamond Head.
Are you going, Wilson?
Yeah, I'll be there.
Yes, oh, Wilson will be there.
Wilson, you'll be buying the beer, Wilson, won't you?
Nope.
No, he won't be buying the beer, but the music will be good.
Plug for the gig tomorrow night, Walsall,
along with Diamond Head, and this is Chevy.
Classic track, Blue Oyster Cult from Agents of Fortune,
Don't Fear the Reaper.
For Andy H and all the BOC fans at Dartmouth High,
Not forgetting a gentleman by the name of Paul Dickin.
Andy H. lives at Great Bar in Birmingham.
Also BOC was requested by Anne of Gloucester.
That's all it says here.
Oh, would I wish feedback?
The best of luck for their gig in December.
I certainly will.
Must say hello to a lady by the name of Jenny Crofts.
Messages, please tell her that I still love her,
even after these many weeks we've been apart.
Sender of message, Rob Lewis of [redacted address].
I think that's Lansdowne in Bath.
Best wishes to everybody at Beechin Cliff School, upper sixth.
And prior to that, you heard The Last of Three.
I was lying when I said four, from Chevy, I beg your pardon.
The Last of Three from Chevy tonight.
The track was called You Got Me Running.
Chevy R, Martin Cure on the vocals,
Paul Shanahan and Steve Warwin on the lead guitars,
Bob Poole on the bass guitar, and Andy Chaplin on the drums.
All the tracks you heard tonight by Chevy are on their album called The Takeout.
Back into the archives.
January 1970, more Yes.
And that was Yes, as if you didn't know.

I was to say to get much higher
[Music]
And that's it for this edition of the Friday Rock Show.
Do not miss Rock on Saturday tomorrow,
an opportunity to hear the interview between Andy Peebles and John Lennon
that was recorded last week in New York.
That will start at about 4.31 on Rock on Saturday tomorrow.
Many thanks to my engineer tonight, who was John.
Following the time term for midnight, the 275-285 transmitters will be closing down.
And now here's Tony Wilson to speak.
Is Friday night music night or what, eh?
Oh, well, whatcha what, well, what, what?
Saturday night could be music night as well
if you join us along with Diamond Head of Warsaw Town Hall.
Thank you very much for the plug.
this is Tommy Vance in London
wherever you are
we wish you well
we really do
goodnight
and God bless
